lines.speaker: FRIAR JOHN
lines.text: Holy Franciscan friar! brother, ho!

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: This same should be the voice of Friar John.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Welcome from Mantua: what says Romeo?

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Or, if his mind be writ, give me his letter.

lines.speaker: FRIAR JOHN
lines.text: Going to find a bare-foot brother out

lines.speaker: FRIAR JOHN
lines.text: One of our order, to associate me,

lines.speaker: FRIAR JOHN
lines.text: Here in this city visiting the sick,

lines.speaker: FRIAR JOHN
lines.text: And finding him, the searchers of the town,

lines.speaker: FRIAR JOHN
lines.text: Suspecting that we both were in a house

lines.speaker: FRIAR JOHN
lines.text: Where the infectious pestilence did reign,

lines.speaker: FRIAR JOHN
lines.text: Seal’d up the doors, and would not let us forth;

lines.speaker: FRIAR JOHN
lines.text: So that my speed to Mantua there was stay’d.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Who bare my letter, then, to Romeo?

lines.speaker: FRIAR JOHN
lines.text: I could not send it—here it is again—

lines.speaker: FRIAR JOHN
lines.text: Nor get a messenger to bring it thee,

lines.speaker: FRIAR JOHN
lines.text: So fearful were they of infection.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Unhappy fortune! by my brotherhood,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: The letter was not nice but full of charge

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Of dear import, and the neglecting it

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: May do much danger. Friar John, go hence;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Get me an iron crow, and bring it straight

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Unto my cell.

lines.speaker: FRIAR JOHN
lines.text: Brother, I’ll go and bring it thee.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Now must I to the monument alone;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Within three hours will fair Juliet wake:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: She will beshrew me much that Romeo

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Hath had no notice of these accidents;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: But I will write again to Mantua,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And keep her at my cell till Romeo come;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Poor living corse, closed in a dead man’s tomb!

glosses.definition: Friar John returns with the undelivered letter
Franciscan friar — the order wore sandals
accompany; the Franciscan rule required going in pairs
the plague — the quarantine that blocked the letter's delivery
iron crowbar — to pry open the tomb
Juliet is alive but entombed — the Friar's compressed tragedy

annotations.title: Plague quarantine — the missed letter
annotations.body: Friar John returns with the undelivered letter. The explanation is appallingly specific: he went to find a fellow Franciscan to accompany him (the Franciscan rule required pairs for travel); the pair entered a house under plague quarantine; they were detained by the searchers of the town as suspected contagion. The letter never reached Mantua. Shakespeare makes the obstacle precise and material. The tragedy is not the work of a malicious fate but of epidemiological bad luck: plague in Verona in the summer of the play (the real Verona endured plagues repeatedly in the sixteenth century, including outbreaks in 1575 and 1596, the latter just a year or two before the play's composition). The missed letter is the technical hinge that makes the final tragedy inevitable.
sources: Jill L. Levenson, The Oxford Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2000)

trials.prompt: Why did the Friar's letter not reach Romeo in Mantua?
options: Friar John refused to deliver it
Friar John was detained by plague quarantine — the letter never reached Mantua
